EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brush fires affected about 600 acres of land in Lowry Hill and Tide Village in East End. No damage were reported on homes, structures or people. The cause of fire was unknown but arson was suspected.
The St. Thomas Airport Tower reported a distant funnel cloud over waters south of the airport.

A strong high pressure system centered north of the area produced locally breezy conditions across the U.S.V.I.  A spotter in St.Croix reported winds gusts reaching 52 mph at Maria Hill. Winds blew patio furniture and downed small trees.
Virgin Island Radio reported a waterspout south of St. John.
Virgin Island radio reported a funnel cloud about three miles south of St. Thomas.

A person drowned when he was pulled underwater along with another student  by a riptide during a water safety class with nine other students. The other swimmer managed to swim out of the strong current but suffered lacerations.
Brush fires affected about 100 acres near Mount Welcome and Recovery Hill. No damages were reported on structures, homes or people. The suspected cause of the fire was an abandon car in flames.

A  brush fire formed at King Still Area across the Centerline Road. The fire affected with smoke, a nearby elementary school.  Four students were taken to the Hospital with respiratory difficulties. All of them were unharmed.
A pilot sighted a waterspout about 12 miles north of St.Thomas. Movement was unknown.

Brush fires affected about 215 acres of land in Recovery Welcome,  Peter's Farm and a section just east of Gallows Bay.  No damages were reported on homes, structure or people.  The cause of  these fires were unknown, but arson was suspected in Gallows Bay.

A strong mid to upper level trough moved across the U.S. Virgin Islands between May7-8.  This system produced torrential rains causing widespread flooding of streams and streets. The hardest hit area was St. Croix where spotters reported between 6 to 12 inches of rain in a 24 hour period.  Rainfall was beneficial to St. Croix since farmers were suffering the effects of a dry spell since the beginning of the 2001.

The Montserrat Volcano erupted during the late afternoon of Sunday July 29.  A large amount of  volcanic ash was released into the atmosphere during Sunday evening and night.  Simultaneously, a strong tropical wave was crossing the Leeward Islands. Strong southeast winds moved the volcanic ash towards the U.S. Virgin Islands and Puerto Rico. Rainfall during Sunday night and Monday morning deposited the ash across most of the Virgin Islands and eastern Puerto Rico.  This was a significant event.  Roads, cars and vegetation were covered by an ash layer.  Aviation operations across Puerto Rico and the Virgin Islands were suspended until Tuesday morning.

CZ_NAME: ST. THOMAS / ST. JOHN AND ADJACENT ISLANDS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical storm Dean formed from a tropical wave in the vicinity of the Virgin Islands on Wednesday August 22.  The storm system moved northwest to north during the day and Thursday morning while weakening. The U.S. Virgin Islands suffered minor damages during the path of the storm. Heavy rains and gusty winds caused power outages.  Minor flooding was reported on the island of St. Croix.  Small trees and branches were blown down in St. Croix and St. John and some roads damage was reported in St. John.   At the St. Thomas airport the maximum sustained wind was 40 mph at 218  pm on  August 22 and the peak gust was 48 mph at 217 pm. The 24 hour rainfall report ending at 800 am on the 23 of August was 1.07 inches.  At the St. Croix airport the maximum sustained wind was 32 mph at 124  pm on August 22 and the peak gust was 47 mph at 137 pm.  The 24 hour rainfall report  ending at 800 am on the 23 of August was 0.49 inches.

A spotter in St. Croix reported winds gust of 71 mph associated with a thunderstorm.
A cold front became stationary over the U.S. Virgin Islands on Thursday bringing heavy rainfall across the islands, especially St. Croix.  Flooding of streets and low lying areas was reported.
Flight Service Station received a report from an aircraft pilot of a waterspout  about 20 miles northwest of St. Croix.
